title: Regime-specialist ensemble with local calibration instead of one global model
summary: F1 pit logic differs across dry vs wet, early vs late race, and first-stint vs later-stint regimes.
rationale: F1 pit logic differs across dry vs wet, early vs late race, and first-stint vs later-stint regimes. Calibration literature shows global Platt/isotonic can miss region-specific distortions, while localized calibration trees can outperform single global calibrators; LightGBM also warns imbalance tricks can hurt probability quality.
implementation_hint: Train 3-5 specialists on disjoint regimes such as dry first-stint, dry later-stint, wet/intermediate, and late-race mandatory-change pressure. Generate OOF predictions, stack with a simple meta-model, then apply regime-conditional beta/isotonic calibration or a lightweight local calibrator using phase/compound as calibration features.
expected_effect: Usually small but real gain from better regime fit and cleaner probabilities, especially if current model is averaging incompatible strategy patterns.
risk: Specialists reduce sample size and can overfit rare wet regimes unless heavily regularized and validated with strict OOF pipelines.
